description: Focus on team dynamics, influence, and leadership contexts

questions.scenario: You are leading a team and realize you have been making decisions based on what will make you look good, not what is best for the project.

options.text: Continue with your current approach since reputation matters
Acknowledge your ego-driven pattern and refocus on project outcomes
Ask your team what they think is best to spread accountability
Justify your decisions as what the organization expects from leaders

questions.scenario: After a successful project, you notice yourself dismissing contributions from team members and taking more credit than you deserve in your mind.

options.text: Enjoy the success - you earned it as the leader
Notice your credit-hogging tendency and actively acknowledge others' contributions
Rationalize that leaders naturally get more credit
Publicly share credit but privately feel you deserved more

questions.scenario: You feel threatened by a high-performing team member who is getting attention from senior leadership.

options.text: Find ways to highlight their mistakes to balance perception
Recognize your insecurity and choose to mentor them instead
Compete harder to prove your own value
Distance yourself from them to protect your position

questions.scenario: You have been avoiding a difficult conversation with an underperforming team member for three weeks.

options.text: Hope the problem resolves itself without intervention
Explore what fear or discomfort is driving your avoidance
Delegate the conversation to HR or your manager
Schedule the conversation and prepare talking points

questions.scenario: You realize you have different standards for different team members - you are harder on some and make excuses for others.

options.text: Continue since different people need different management styles
Examine your biases and establish consistent criteria for everyone
Be harder on everyone to appear fair
Ask your manager if they notice your inconsistency

questions.scenario: You feel a rush of excitement when a competitor fails, even though it does not directly help your company.

options.text: Celebrate privately - business is competitive
Notice that your joy comes from their pain, not your gain
Share the news with your team to boost morale
Use their failure as a learning opportunity for your team

questions.scenario: Your team misses a critical deadline. You want to blame external factors but know you also made mistakes.

options.text: Present the failure as entirely due to circumstances beyond your control
Acknowledge your part while also addressing systemic issues
Take full blame publicly to show accountability
Analyze what went wrong privately before discussing with stakeholders

questions.scenario: A board member publicly challenges your strategy in a meeting. You feel your authority being undermined.

options.text: Defend your strategy aggressively to reassert authority
Regulate your defensive reaction and engage with their concerns
Defer to their experience and change your strategy immediately
Schedule a private meeting to understand their concerns better

questions.scenario: You receive anonymous negative feedback about your leadership style. You suspect who wrote it and feel angry.

options.text: Confront the person you suspect and demand an explanation
Process your anger and look for any truth in the feedback
Dismiss it as anonymous cowardice
Send a team-wide message inviting direct feedback

questions.scenario: Your company is going through layoffs. You know information that would calm your team but have been told to keep it confidential.

options.text: Hint at the good news to reduce your team's anxiety
Maintain confidentiality while supporting your team through uncertainty
Tell your team everything to build trust
Push back on leadership to allow earlier communication

questions.scenario: A key investor is pressuring you to make an unethical short-term decision that would harm your team long-term.

options.text: Comply to keep the investor happy and your company funded
Stand firm on your values while seeking alternative solutions
Find a compromise that partially satisfies both sides
Delay the decision hoping the investor changes their mind

questions.scenario: Your team is celebrating a win but you know there were serious ethical shortcuts taken to achieve it.

options.text: Join the celebration and address the issues later
Pause the celebration to address what went wrong
Celebrate publicly but privately investigate the shortcuts
Report the shortcuts to compliance without telling your team

questions.scenario: A team member is consistently underperforming. Other team members are picking up the slack and resenting it.

options.text: Focus on the impact to the team and terminate them quickly
Explore what might be causing the underperformance before taking action
Ignore it and hope the team works it out themselves
Reassign their work to others permanently

questions.scenario: A high performer on your team asks for a raise you cannot approve due to budget constraints.

options.text: Reject the request and tell them to be grateful for their job
Validate their worth and explore alternative forms of recognition
Promise to advocate for them in the next budget cycle
Suggest they look for other offers to benchmark their value

questions.scenario: During a restructuring, you need to choose between two equally qualified employees for one remaining position.

options.text: Make the decision purely on metrics without considering personal impact
Consider both business needs and individual circumstances transparently
Let them compete for the role to make it fair
Delegate the decision to avoid personal responsibility

questions.scenario: A team member shares they are experiencing burnout but there is critical work that only they can do.

options.text: Tell them to push through until the critical work is done
Take their burnout seriously and redistribute work immediately
Offer them a long weekend after the critical work is complete
Suggest they see a doctor to get a medical leave note

questions.scenario: You notice that your team is divided into two factions that barely communicate with each other.

options.text: Let them self-organize since they are adults
Seek to understand each faction's perspective and bridge the divide
Force them to work together on a high-pressure project
Reorganize the teams to separate the factions

questions.scenario: An employee confides in you about a personal crisis affecting their work. You have a tight deadline approaching.

options.text: Focus on the deadline and suggest they use PTO if they cannot perform
Support them while also being transparent about business needs
Cover for them and pick up their work yourself
Refer them to HR and focus on your own responsibilities

questions.scenario: Your team is demotivated after a series of setbacks. Energy is low and cynicism is high.

options.text: Tell them to toughen up and focus on the work
Acknowledge the difficulty and co-create a path forward with the team
Bring in motivational speakers to pump up the team
Share your own struggles to show vulnerability

questions.scenario: You need to implement an unpopular policy change that comes from senior leadership.

options.text: Present it as non-negotiable and enforce compliance
Explain the why behind it and create space for feedback within constraints
Blame senior leadership to maintain your popularity
Delay implementation hoping leadership changes their mind

questions.scenario: A talented team member wants to leave for another opportunity. You could make a counteroffer but are not sure if you should.

options.text: Let them go immediately since they are disloyal
Have an honest conversation about their growth and whether you can meet it
Make a counteroffer without discussing their underlying needs
Ask them to stay as a personal favor to you

questions.scenario: Two of your strongest performers are in conflict that is affecting the whole team.

options.text: Tell them to resolve it themselves or face consequences
Facilitate a mediated conversation to understand and resolve the root cause
Separate them onto different projects to avoid future conflict
Pick a side based on who is more valuable to the team

questions.scenario: You need to build buy-in for a strategic shift that many on your team resist.

options.text: Announce the decision and expect compliance
Involve key influencers early and address concerns transparently
Gradually implement changes hoping people adapt over time
Find early wins to demonstrate the value of the shift

questions.scenario: You are new to an organization and need to establish credibility with a team that has seen three leaders in two years.

options.text: Make quick changes to show you are different from previous leaders
Listen extensively before acting and acknowledge their history of instability
Promise you will stay longer than the others did
Focus on delivering early results to build trust quickly
